You are working inside Conductor, a Mac app that lets the user run many coding agents in parallel.
Your work should take place in the /Users/fcamblor/conductor/workspaces/mac-ai-trackers/warsaw directory (unless otherwise directed), which has been set up for you to work in.
Each workspace has a .context directory (gitignored) where you can save files to collaborate with other agents.
The target branch for this workspace is origin/copilot-cli-usage-api-research. Use this for actions like diffing (`git diff origin/copilot-cli-usage-api-research...`) or creating PRs (`gh pr create --base copilot-cli-usage-api-research`).

Do not rename the current branch unless the user explicitly tells you to do so.

By default, the user will only see the last message that you send before stopping. Include all essential information in the last message. The intermediate messages will be collapsed and accessible by the user but not displayed by default.

If the user asks you to work on several unrelated tasks, you can suggest they start new workspaces.
Sometimes the user might send you a message they meant to send in a different workspace or a different chat. If something doesn't make sense in the context of your work, just ask. 
If the user asks for help with Conductor, you can ask them to go to "Help -> Send Feedback" to get in touch with our team.
</system_instruction>

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: Before using any chrome browser tools, you MUST first load them using ToolSearch.**

Chrome browser tools are MCP tools that require loading before use. Before calling any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tool:
1. Use ToolSearch with `select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__<tool_name>` to load the specific tool
2. Then call the tool

For example, to get tab context:
1. First: ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp"
2. Then: Call mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## plugin:serena:serena
CRITICAL: Before starting to work on a coding task, call the `initial_instructions` tool to read the 'Serena Instructions Manual'.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- animate: Review a feature and enhance it with purposeful animations, micro-interactions, and motion effects that improve usability and delight. Use when the user mentions adding animation, transitions, micro-interactions, motion design, hover effects, or making the UI feel more alive.
- quieter: Tones down visually aggressive or overstimulating designs, reducing intensity while preserving quality. Use when the user mentions too bold, too loud, overwhelming, aggressive, garish, or wants a calmer, more refined aesthetic.
- archon: Use when: User wants to run Archon workflows, CREATE workflows or commands,
set up Archon, or manage Archon configuration.
Triggers (run): "use archon to", "run archon", "archon workflow", "use archon for",
          "have archon", "let archon", "ask archon to".
Triggers (create): "create a workflow", "write a workflow", "make a command",
          "author a workflow", "new workflow", "new command", "archon workflow yaml".
Triggers (setup): "set up archon", "install archon", "how to use archon",
          "configure archon", "archon setup", "get started with archon".
Triggers (config): "change my archon config", "modify archon config", "archon config",
          "change archon settings", "update my config", "help me change my config",
          "edit archon config", "archon configuration".
Triggers (init): "initialize archon", "set up .archon", "archon init", "add archon to repo".
Capability: Runs AI workflows in isolated git worktrees for parallel development.
Also: Creates and manages workflow YAML files, command files, and configuration.
NOT for: Direct Claude Code work - only for delegating to Archon CLI.
- shape: Plan the UX and UI for a feature before writing code. Runs a structured discovery interview, then produces a design brief that guides implementation. Use during the planning phase to establish design direction, constraints, and strategy before any code is written.
- optimize: Diagnoses and fixes UI performance across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size. Use when the user mentions slow, laggy, janky, performance, bundle size, load time, or wants a faster, smoother experience.
- adapt: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Implements breakpoints, fluid layouts, and touch targets. Use when the user mentions responsive design, mobile layouts, breakpoints, viewport adaptation, or cross-device compatibility.
- impeccable: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics. Use when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications, or when any design skill requires project context. Call with 'craft' for shape-then-build, 'teach' for design context setup, or 'extract' to pull reusable components and tokens into the design system.
- clarify: Improve unclear UX copy, error messages, microcopy, labels, and instructions to make interfaces easier to understand. Use when the user mentions confusing text, unclear labels, bad error messages, hard-to-follow instructions, or wanting better UX writing.
- layout: Improve layout, spacing, and visual rhythm. Fixes monotonous grids, inconsistent spacing, and weak visual hierarchy. Use when the user mentions layout feeling off, spacing issues, visual hierarchy, crowded UI, alignment problems, or wanting better composition.
- dbg: Debug applications using the dbg CLI debugger. Supports Node.js (V8/CDP), Bun (WebKit/JSC), and native code via LLDB (DAP). Use when: (1) investigating runtime bugs by stepping through code, (2) inspecting variable values at specific execution points, (3) setting breakpoints and conditional breakpoints, (4) evaluating expressions in a paused context, (5) hot-patching code without restarting (JS/TS), (6) debugging test failures by attaching to a running process, (7) debugging C/C++/Rust/Swift with LLDB, (8) any task where understanding runtime behavior requires a debugger. Triggers: "debug this", "set a breakpoint", "step through", "inspect variables", "why is this value wrong", "trace execution", "attach debugger", "runtime error", "segfault", "core dump".
- distill: Strip designs to their essence by removing unnecessary complexity. Great design is simple, powerful, and clean. Use when the user asks to simplify, declutter, reduce noise, remove elements, or make a UI cleaner and more focused.
- delight: Add moments of joy, personality, and unexpected touches that make interfaces memorable and enjoyable to use. Elevates functional to delightful. Use when the user asks to add polish, personality, animations, micro-interactions, delight, or make an interface feel fun or memorable.
- audit: Run technical quality checks across accessibility, performance, theming, responsive design, and anti-patterns. Generates a scored report with P0-P3 severity ratings and actionable plan. Use when the user wants an accessibility check, performance audit, or technical quality review.
- polish: Performs a final quality pass fixing alignment, spacing, consistency, and micro-detail issues before shipping. Use when the user mentions polish, finishing touches, pre-launch review, something looks off, or wants to go from good to great.
- bolder: Amplify safe or boring designs to make them more visually interesting and stimulating. Increases impact while maintaining usability. Use when the user says the design looks bland, generic, too safe, lacks personality, or wants more visual impact and character.
- typeset: Improves typography by fixing font choices, hierarchy, sizing, weight, and readability so text feels intentional. Use when the user mentions fonts, type, readability, text hierarchy, sizing looks off, or wants more polished, intentional typography.
- critique: Evaluate design from a UX perspective, assessing visual hierarchy, information architecture, emotional resonance, cognitive load, and overall quality with quantitative scoring, persona-based testing, automated anti-pattern detection, and actionable feedback. Use when the user asks to review, critique, evaluate, or give feedback on a design or component.
- colorize: Add strategic color to features that are too monochromatic or lack visual interest, making interfaces more engaging and expressive. Use when the user mentions the design looking gray, dull, lacking warmth, needing more color, or wanting a more vibrant or expressive palette.
- overdrive: Pushes interfaces past conventional limits with technically ambitious implementations — shaders, spring physics, scroll-driven reveals, 60fps animations. Use when the user wants to wow, impress, go all-out, or make something that feels extraordinary.
- assistant: Routes to the correct sub-skill of the assistant family based on the issue's type:* and phase:* labels. Use when the user mentions a vendor onboarding or evolution and is unsure which skill to run.
- assistant-review: Review an open assistant PR against the dual-track checklist (dated documentation first, then code), with per-issue-type addenda for new vendors vs evolution. Use when the issue is at phase:review.
- tech-debt: Create a new technical debt entry in the tech-debt/ directory. Use when identifying technical debt to formalize, track, or document for future resolution.
- assistant-release: Officialize a merged assistant change by updating README (new-assistant only), docs/vendors/index, and crediting testers in release notes; close the issue. Use after a tagged release ships the work.
- improve-ai-harness: Analyze review feedbacks or development friction points and suggest improvements to the AI harness (rules, CLAUDE.md, docs, tooling). Use when asked to improve AI rules, after a code review with issues, or to strengthen the AI configuration.
- assistant-implement: Scaffold (new-assistant) or refactor (vendor-evolution), document, test, and open a draft PR. Use when the issue is at phase:approved or phase:implementing and the contributor is starting or resuming work.
- assistant-merge: Verify gates one last time and squash-merge an assistant PR (new-assistant or vendor-evolution). Use when the issue is at phase:merge-ready.
- roadmap-feature: Create a new epic entry in the roadmap/ directory. Use when the user wants to plan a new feature, add an item to the roadmap, or formalize an epic for future implementation.
- assistant-reverify: Re-verify a vendor's API behavior against its docs/vendors/<vendor>.md snapshot, bump dates, append a Change log entry, and either open a doc-only PR (no drift impact) or file a type:vendor-evolution issue (drift forces connector changes). Use when API drift is suspected.
- assistant-triage: Help the maintainer triage a phase:proposed assistant issue (new-assistant or vendor-evolution) — duplicate check, scope sanity, kind:* label application, decision draft. Use when reviewing an incoming proposal.
- assistant-tester-followup: Tally tester sign-off comments on a phase:testing issue, validate them against the latest build SHA, audit any attached connector logs for sanitization gaps, and draft follow-ups for incomplete confirmations. Use whenever a tester comments.
- session-save: Save the current session state — decisions, modified files, current status, and next steps — to a handoff file for later resume.
- learn-from-mistakes: Now that you learnt some new stuff, write detailed specs and reduce ambiguity before handing work...
- commit: Commit les changements du workspace git avec guidelines opinionated (conventional commits détectés, langue détectée, fixup automatique)
- pr-context: Load the full context of a GitHub Pull Request so the user can ask questions about it.
- log-4sh-time:log-4sh-time: Interactive workflow to log work time in 4track with semantic task matching. View aggregated work statistics by project, perimeter, and task. Use this skill when the user wants to create a worklog, log hours, track time, record work activities, or analyze time spent on tasks.
- typescript-standards:enforce-ts-standards: Enforce type-safe TypeScript patterns with Zod validation, pattern matching, and discriminated unions to prevent runtime errors. Use when writing TypeScript code, reviewing for type safety, designing data validation flows, or refactoring to eliminate impossible states.
- oss-security-audit:audit-oss-package: Security audit of a third-party package (npm, pip, homebrew, cargo, gem…) before
using it. Fetches its source code, scans for malicious or suspicious patterns
(outbound network calls, telemetry, eval/exec, install hooks, opaque file writes,
supply-chain risks), then recursively audits same-author dependencies found in the
package manifest.
Use when the user says "is this package safe", "audit this dependency",
"check this npm/pip/brew package", "can I trust this library", or asks
whether a package is trustworthy before adding it to a project.
- impeccable:quieter: Tones down visually aggressive or overstimulating designs, reducing intensity while preserving quality. Use when the user mentions too bold, too loud, overwhelming, aggressive, garish, or wants a calmer, more refined aesthetic.
- impeccable:animate: Review a feature and enhance it with purposeful animations, micro-interactions, and motion effects that improve usability and delight. Use when the user mentions adding animation, transitions, micro-interactions, motion design, hover effects, or making the UI feel more alive.

Use when the user asks to harden, make production-ready, handle edge cases, add error states, design empty states, improve onboarding, or fix overflow and i18n issues.

Use when the user asks to review, critique, evaluate, or give feedback on a design or component.

159	    private func setupUpdateScheduler() {
160	        // In a development build (swift run) there's no Info.plist version, so
161	        // we fall back to 0.0.0 — every release will then be reported as newer,
162	        // but the existing "Skip this version" flow ensures the alert isn't
163	        // re-shown until a different version ships.
164	        let effectiveVersion = Self.currentAppVersion() ?? AppVersion(string: "0.0.0")!
165	        let bundlePath = Bundle.main.bundleURL.path
166	        let detector = InstallationDetector(bundlePath: bundlePath)
167	        self.installationDetector = detector
168	        self.updateInstaller = UpdateInstaller()
169	        self.updateDownloader = UpdateDownloader()
170	        self.brewUpgradeRunner = BrewUpgradeRunner()
171	        let scheduler = UpdateScheduler(
172	            checker: UpdateChecker(),
173	            detector: detector,
174	            currentVersion: effectiveVersion,
175	            preferencesAccessor: { Self.sharedPreferences },
176	            stateAccessor: { Self.sharedUpdateState },
177	            onUpdateAvailable: { [weak self] update, kind in
178	                self?.presentUpdateAlert(update: update, kind: kind)
179	            }
180	        )
181	        self.updateScheduler = scheduler
182	        Self.sharedUpdateScheduler = scheduler
183	        Self.sharedTriggerUpdateInstall = { [weak self] in
184	            self?.triggerUpdateInstall()
185	        }
186	        Self.sharedTriggerRestart = { [weak self] in
187	            self?.triggerRestart()
188	        }
189	    }
190	
191	    static func currentAppVersion() -> AppVersion? {
192	        guard let raw = Bundle.main.object(forInfoDictionaryKey: "CFBundleShortVersionString") as? String else {
193	            return nil
194	        }
195	        return AppVersion(string: raw)
196	    }
197	
198	    /// Human-readable label for the running app version, falling back to a
199	    /// "development build" marker when launched via `swift run` (no Info.plist).
200	    static func currentAppVersionLabel() -> String {
201	        currentAppVersion()?.rawValue ?? "development build"
202	    }

205	        quit()

content.thinking: I need to check how CodexConnector determines the active account and sets the isActive flag.

I'm seeing how CodexConnector always marks accounts as active when fetching them, while CodexActiveAccountMonitor watches for account_id changes in auth.json to trigger cache invalidation and polling. The monitor only fires when the account switches, not when files are read, so I need to trace through UsagesFileManager's merge logic to understand why the active account keeps alternating.

I'm noticing the merge logic upserts records by vendor and account but doesn't deactivate isActive on other accounts from the same vendor, which means multiple accounts could end up marked as active. Let me check how the Claude connector handles this to see if it's a consistent pattern.

200	    // MARK: - Response parsing
201	
202	    private static let sensitiveKeyPatterns = ["token", "key", "secret", "password", "email", "credential"]
203	
204	    private static func maskedPayload(_ data: Data) -> String {
205	        guard let json = try? JSONSerialization.jsonObject(with: data) as? [String: Any] else {
206	            return "<non-JSON, \(data.count) bytes>"
207	        }
208	        let masked = maskSensitiveFields(json)
209	        guard let out = try? JSONSerialization.data(withJSONObject: masked, options: [.sortedKeys]),
210	              let str = String(data: out, encoding: .utf8) else {
211	            return "<serialization failed>"
212	        }
213	        return str
214	    }
215	
216	    private static func maskSensitiveFields(_ dict: [String: Any]) -> [String: Any] {
217	        var result: [String: Any] = [:]
218	        for (key, value) in dict {
219	            let lower = key.lowercased()
220	            if sensitiveKeyPatterns.contains(where: { lower.contains($0) }) {
221	                result[key] = "***"
222	            } else if let nested = value as? [String: Any] {
223	                result[key] = maskSensitiveFields(nested)
224	            } else if let array = value as? [[String: Any]] {
225	                result[key] = array.map { maskSensitiveFields($0) }
226	            } else {
227	                result[key] = value
228	            }
229	        }
230	        return result
231	    }
232	
233	    private struct ModelWeeklyMetric {
234	        let percent: Int
235	        let resetAt: ISODate?
236	    }
237	
238	    private struct ParsedUsage {
239	        // Every window is optional: a freshly provisioned account (or an account
240	        // between reset cycles) returns `utilization: 0, resets_at: null` and must
241	        // not be treated as a parse failure.
242	        let sessionPercent: Int?
243	        let sessionResetAt: ISODate?
244	        let weeklyPercent: Int?
245	        let weeklyResetAt: ISODate?
246	        let sonnetWeekly: ModelWeeklyMetric?
247	        let opusWeekly: ModelWeeklyMetric?
248	    }
249	
250	    private func parseAPIResponse(_ data: Data) throws -> ParsedUsage {
251	        let jsonObject = try JSONSerialization.jsonObject(with: data)
252	        guard let json = jsonObject as? [String: Any] else {
253	            throw ConnectorError.unexpectedAPIFormat(receivedKeys: [])
254	        }
255	        // Unknown top-level keys are ignored by design — the API evolves with experimental
256	        // fields (e.g. `omelette_promotional`, `iguana_necktie`) and we only consume the
257	        // windows we know about. Only a truly malformed payload (no known window at all)
258	        // escalates to an error.
259	        let session = extractOptionalModelMetric(from: json, key: "five_hour")
260	        let weekly = extractOptionalModelMetric(from: json, key: "seven_day")
261	        let sonnet = extractOptionalModelMetric(from: json, key: "seven_day_sonnet")
262	        let opus = extractOptionalModelMetric(from: json, key: "seven_day_opus")
263	
264	        let hasFiveHourBlock = json["five_hour"] is [String: Any]
265	        let hasSevenDayBlock = json["seven_day"] is [String: Any]
266	        if !hasFiveHourBlock, !hasSevenDayBlock {
267	            logger.log(.warning, "No known usage window in payload — top-level keys: \(Array(json.keys))")
268	            throw ConnectorError.unexpectedAPIFormat(receivedKeys: Array(json.keys))
269	        }
270	
271	        return ParsedUsage(
272	            sessionPercent: session.map { Int($0.percent) },
273	            sessionResetAt: session.flatMap { $0.resetAt.map { normalizeISO8601($0) } },
274	            weeklyPercent: weekly.map { Int($0.percent) },
275	            weeklyResetAt: weekly.flatMap { $0.resetAt.map { normalizeISO8601($0) } },
276	            sonnetWeekly: sonnet.map { ModelWeeklyMetric(percent: $0.percent, resetAt: $0.resetAt.map { normalizeISO8601($0) }) },
277	            opusWeekly: opus.map { ModelWeeklyMetric(percent: $0.percent, resetAt: $0.resetAt.map { normalizeISO8601($0) }) }
278	        )
279	    }
280	
281	    /// Every usage window block is optional — a missing key or an unexpected shape is silently
282	    /// skipped. A null `resets_at` (fresh account, between cycles) is kept with `resetAt: nil`
283	    /// so the metric still surfaces in the UI as "???".
284	    private func extractOptionalModelMetric(from json: [String: Any], key: String) -> (percent: Int, resetAt: String?)? {
285	        guard let block = json[key] as? [String: Any] else {
286	            return nil
287	        }
288	        guard let util = block["utilization"] as? Double else {
289	            logger.log(.debug, "Block '\(key)' present but utilization missing — skipped")
290	            return nil
291	        }
292	        let resetAt = block["resets_at"] as? String
293	        if resetAt == nil {
294	            logger.log(.debug, "Block '\(key)' has null resets_at — metric emitted without reset date")
295	        }
296	        return (percent: Int(util.rounded()), resetAt: resetAt)
297	    }

299	    /// Strips sub-second precision from API-provided ISO8601 strings so downstream
300	    /// consumers can rely on the standard formatter (no fractional-seconds option needed).
301	    /// Returns the original string unchanged if it cannot be parsed.
302	    private func normalizeISO8601(_ raw: String) -> ISODate {
303	        if let date = isoFormatterFractional.date(from: raw) ?? isoFormatter.date(from: raw) {
304	            return ISODate(rawValue: isoFormatter.string(from: date))
305	        }
306	        return ISODate(rawValue: raw)
307	    }

content.thinking: I see the issue now—both connectors mark their fetched account as active, but the merge logic never deactivates the previous accounts from the same vendor. The `updateIsActive` method exists to handle this, but nothing's actually calling it.

I found the issue — `updateIsActive` is being called in `ClaudeActiveAccountMonitor` but not in `CodexActiveAccountMonitor`, so I need to check how the Claude version implements it to understand what's missing.

I'm seeing the difference now — `ClaudeActiveAccountMonitor` explicitly calls `fileManager.updateIsActive()` to deactivate other accounts, while `CodexActiveAccountMonitor` only triggers the callback without that deactivation step. This means multiple Codex accounts can remain marked as active simultaneously after their connectors fetch, which might explain the alternating behavior the user mentioned.

Author: Frédéric Camblor <fcamblor@gmail.com>
Date:   Thu May 7 03:15:48 2026 +0200

    refactor(vendor-plugin): migrate Codex and Copilot onto contract
    
    Renames CodexAuth/CodexAuthProviding/CodexAuthError to
    CodexCredentialLocator/CodexCredentialLocating/CodexCredentialLocatorError
    and the legacy load() to the contract's locate() — same for Copilot.
    Both protocols inherit from CredentialLocator with their respective
    Credentials associated type, so the connectors thread vendor-specific
    shapes through the shared abstraction. Adds CodexPayloadSanitizer (with
    exact-key redaction for user_id / account_id and JWT-shape patterns)
    and CopilotPayloadSanitizer (preserving the public login while redacting
    analytics_tracking_id and stripping gh* / Bearer / go-keyring-base64
    patterns), plus their fixtures and leakage tests. Wires both
    active-account monitors onto ActiveAccountMonitoring and introduces
    CodexPlugin / CopilotCLIPlugin namespaces that build and register their
    VendorBundles with VendorRegistry. AppDelegate is unchanged so far —
    existing call sites still drive the connectors directly.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

content.thinking:
I'm realizing the Codex monitor only triggers the change callback without calling `updateIsActive` directly—the connector itself sets `isActive: true` on success. So with multiple Codex accounts, they'd all remain active, but the user is seeing them alternate, which suggests maybe the connector returns one entry per fetch while the file retains previous accounts, or there's something about the initial setup that's causing this behavior.

Looking at the merge logic, when the connector returns CodexAccountB with `isActive: true`, it gets upserted while CodexAccountA stays in the file with its last state—both should stay active. But if the user is seeing alternation between Codex and Claude, maybe it's because the poller only fetches one connector at a time, so only one account appears active during each poll cycle.

I'm realizing the issue — the Codex connector only returns `isActive: true` on successful requests, but when it encounters errors like token failures or API issues, it defaults to returning `isActive: false`, which would explain why the status appears flaky if the connector is intermittently failing. Let me trace through the specific error paths to see which ones are setting this flag.

Looking at the git history to understand what actually changed between versions, I notice the refactor switched from using a plain `String` for the account ID to a typed `AccountId` — there might be something else in that diff that's causing the behavior shift.

content.thinking:
Looking back at the original codex monitor code, it never actually called `updateIsActive` — it only triggered the callback. So this isn't a regression introduced by the plugin generalization. But the user reported seeing a regression, which suggests the issue might be intermittent, possibly due to the codex API failing or the connector returning incomplete data.

Let me trace through what happens when the connector merges results. It returns one entry with `active=true` for the current account on success, but it doesn't touch other existing Codex accounts in the file. If this is a fresh test build with just one account, there shouldn't be any conflicts. I'm wondering if the "active" badge computation in the UI itself is the culprit — let me check how AccountCardView determines what gets marked as active.

content.thinking:
The user is saying the behavior alternates, possibly between different Codex accounts or instances. I should dig into the connector code to understand what's happening—the Codex `fetchUsages()` function only returns a single account entry at a time, and it sets `isActive=true` on success, but something's cutting off the logic for other states.

Looking at the error handling, I'm realizing the toggle between active and inactive states depends on whether the API call succeeds or fails. If the Codex API is flaky or timing out, that would explain the alternating behavior. But there's another possibility—maybe the `resolvePersistedAccount` function is returning nil in some cases (when there's no email in the response and no cached credentials), which would mean no entry gets written that tick, leaving the old entry in place. That could create the alternating pattern the user is seeing.

Let me trace through the credential resolution logic more carefully to understand what's actually stored in the CodexCredentials object and how it falls back through the email resolution chain.

content.content: AIUsagesTrackers/Sources/AIUsagesTrackers/Connectors/CodexCredentialLocator.swift

1	import Foundation
2	
3	// MARK: - Credentials
4	
5	public struct CodexCredentials: Sendable, Equatable {
6	    public let accessToken: String
7	    public let accountId: AccountId
8	    public let accountEmail: AccountEmail?
9	    /// Parsed from `last_refresh` in auth.json; nil when the field is absent or unparseable.
10	    public let lastRefreshedAt: Date?
11	
12	    public init(
13	        accessToken: String,
14	        accountId: AccountId,
15	        accountEmail: AccountEmail? = nil,
16	        lastRefreshedAt: Date? = nil
17	    ) {
18	        self.accessToken = accessToken
19	        self.accountId = accountId
20	        self.accountEmail = accountEmail
21	        self.lastRefreshedAt = lastRefreshedAt
22	    }
23	
24	    public init(
25	        accessToken: String,
26	        accountId: String,
27	        accountEmail: AccountEmail? = nil,
28	        lastRefreshedAt: Date? = nil
29	    ) {
30	        self.init(
31	            accessToken: accessToken,
32	            accountId: AccountId(rawValue: accountId),
33	            accountEmail: accountEmail,
34	            lastRefreshedAt: lastRefreshedAt
35	        )
36	    }
37	}
38	
39	// MARK: - Protocol
40	
41	public protocol CodexCredentialLocating: CredentialLocator where Credentials == CodexCredentials {}
42	
43	// MARK: - Error
44	
45	public enum CodexCredentialLocatorError: Error, CustomStringConvertible {
46	    case noAuthFileFound(searchedPaths: [String])
47	    case readFailed(path: String, underlying: Error)
48	    case parseFailed(path: String, rawPreview: String)
49	    case missingAccountId(path: String)
50	    case keychainAccessDenied(exitCode: Int32)
51	    case keychainEmpty
52	    case keychainTimeout(timeoutSeconds: Int)
53	    case keychainParseFailed(rawPreview: String)
54	
55	    public var description: String {
56	        switch self {
57	        case let .noAuthFileFound(paths):
58	            "No Codex auth file found — searched: \(paths.joined(separator: ", "))"
59	        case let .readFailed(path, underlying):
60	            "Failed to read Codex auth file at '\(path)': \(underlying)"
61	        case let .parseFailed(path, preview):
62	            "Failed to parse Codex auth JSON at '\(path)' — preview: '\(preview.prefix(80))'"
63	        case let .missingAccountId(path):
64	            "Codex auth at '\(path)' has no tokens.account_id — cannot identify account"
65	        case let .keychainAccessDenied(code):
66	            "Keychain access denied for service 'Codex Auth' (exit \(code))"
67	        case .keychainEmpty:
68	            "Keychain item is empty for service 'Codex Auth'"
69	        case let .keychainTimeout(secs):
70	            "Keychain access timed out after \(secs)s for service 'Codex Auth'"
71	        case let .keychainParseFailed(preview):
72	            "Failed to parse Codex Auth keychain value — preview: '\(preview.prefix(80))'"
73	        }
74	    }
75	}
76	
77	// MARK: - Implementation
78	
79	public actor CodexCredentialLocator: CodexCredentialLocating {
80	    public typealias Credentials = CodexCredentials
81	
82	    private static let keychainService = "Codex Auth"
83	    private static let keychainTimeoutSeconds = 10
84	
85	    private let codexHomePath: String?
86	    private let fileManager: FileManager
87	    private let logger: FileLogger
88	    private let processRunner: ProcessRunning
89	
90	    // Actor-isolated; safe without nonisolated(unsafe)
91	    private let isoFormatter: ISO8601DateFormatter = {
92	        let f = ISO8601DateFormatter()
93	        f.formatOptions = [.withInternetDateTime, .withFractionalSeconds]
94	        return f
95	    }()
96	
97	    public init(
98	        codexHomePath: String? = nil,
99	        fileManager: FileManager = .default,
100	        logger: FileLogger = Loggers.codex,
101	        processRunner: ProcessRunning = FoundationProcessRunner()
102	    ) {
103	        self.codexHomePath = codexHomePath
104	        self.fileManager = fileManager
105	        self.logger = logger
106	        self.processRunner = processRunner
107	    }
108	
109	    public func locate() async throws -> CodexCredentials {
110	        let home = fileManager.homeDirectoryForCurrentUser.path
111	
112	        // Build candidate paths in cascade order
113	        var candidates: [String] = []
114	        if let codexHome = codexHomePath ?? ProcessInfo.processInfo.environment["CODEX_HOME"] {
115	            candidates.append("\(codexHome)/auth.json")
116	        }
117	        candidates.append("\(home)/.config/codex/auth.json")
118	        candidates.append("\(home)/.codex/auth.json")
119	
120	        for path in candidates {
121	            guard fileManager.fileExists(atPath: path) else { continue }
122	            logger.log(.debug, "Loading Codex auth from \(path)")
123	            return try parseAuthFile(at: path)
124	        }
125	
126	        // Keychain fallback
127	        logger.log(.debug, "No auth.json found — falling back to Keychain '\(Self.keychainService)'")
128	        return try await loadFromKeychain()
129	    }
130	
131	    // MARK: - File parsing
132	
133	    private func parseAuthFile(at path: String) throws -> CodexCredentials {
134	        let data: Data
135	        do {
136	            data = try Data(contentsOf: URL(fileURLWithPath: path))
137	        } catch {
138	            throw CodexCredentialLocatorError.readFailed(path: path, underlying: error)
139	        }
140	
141	        let jsonObject: Any
142	        do {
143	            jsonObject = try JSONSerialization.jsonObject(with: data)
144	        } catch {
145	            let preview = String(data: data.prefix(80), encoding: .utf8) ?? "<binary>"
146	            throw CodexCredentialLocatorError.parseFailed(path: path, rawPreview: preview)
147	        }
148	        guard let json = jsonObject as? [String: Any] else {
149	            let preview = String(data: data.prefix(80), encoding: .utf8) ?? "<binary>"
150	            throw CodexCredentialLocatorError.parseFailed(path: path, rawPreview: preview)
151	        }
152	
153	        let tokens = json["tokens"] as? [String: Any]
154	        guard let accessToken = tokens?["access_token"] as? String, !accessToken.isEmpty else {
155	            // Tolerate OPENAI_API_KEY-only auth files: no tokens dict means no account_id either
156	            let preview = String(data: data.prefix(80), encoding: .utf8) ?? "<binary>"
157	            throw CodexCredentialLocatorError.parseFailed(path: path, rawPreview: preview)
158	        }
159	
160	        guard let accountId = tokens?["account_id"] as? String, !accountId.isEmpty else {
161	            throw CodexCredentialLocatorError.missingAccountId(path: path)
162	        }
163	
164	        let lastRefreshedAt: Date?
165	        if let raw = json["last_refresh"] as? String {
166	            lastRefreshedAt = isoFormatter.date(from: raw)
167	        } else {
168	            lastRefreshedAt = nil
169	        }
170	
171	        return CodexCredentials(
172	            accessToken: accessToken,
173	            accountId: AccountId(rawValue: accountId),
174	            accountEmail: extractAccountEmail(from: tokens),
175	            lastRefreshedAt: lastRefreshedAt
176	        )
177	    }
178	
179	    // MARK: - Keychain
180	
181	    private func loadFromKeychain() async throws -> CodexCredentials {
182	        let serviceName = Self.keychainService
183	        let timeoutSecs = Self.keychainTimeoutSeconds
184	        let result = try await processRunner.run(
185	            executablePath: "/usr/bin/security",
186	            arguments: ["find-generic-password", "-s", serviceName, "-w"],
187	            timeoutSeconds: timeoutSecs
188	        )
189	        if result.timedOut {
190	            throw CodexCredentialLocatorError.keychainTimeout(timeoutSeconds: timeoutSecs)
191	        }
192	        guard result.terminationStatus == 0 else {
193	            throw CodexCredentialLocatorError.keychainAccessDenied(exitCode: result.terminationStatus)
194	        }
195	
196	        var rawString = String(data: result.stdout, encoding: .utf8)?.trimmingCharacters(in: .whitespacesAndNewlines) ?? ""
197	        if rawString.isEmpty {
198	            throw CodexCredentialLocatorError.keychainEmpty
199	        }
200	
201	        // Keychain may hex-encode the value with a leading "0x" prefix
202	        if rawString.hasPrefix("0x"), let decoded = Self.decodeHexUtf8(rawString) {
203	            rawString = decoded
204	        }
205	
206	        guard let jsonData = rawString.data(using: .utf8) else {
207	            throw CodexCredentialLocatorError.keychainParseFailed(rawPreview: rawString)
208	        }
209	        let jsonObject: Any
210	        do {
211	            jsonObject = try JSONSerialization.jsonObject(with: jsonData)
212	        } catch {
213	            throw CodexCredentialLocatorError.keychainParseFailed(rawPreview: rawString)
214	        }
215	        guard let json = jsonObject as? [String: Any],
216	              let tokens = json["tokens"] as? [String: Any],
217	              let accessToken = tokens["access_token"] as? String, !accessToken.isEmpty,
218	              let accountId = tokens["account_id"] as? String, !accountId.isEmpty else {
219	            throw CodexCredentialLocatorError.keychainParseFailed(rawPreview: rawString)
220	        }
221	
222	        let lastRefreshedAt: Date?
223	        if let raw = json["last_refresh"] as? String {
224	            lastRefreshedAt = isoFormatter.date(from: raw)
225	        } else {
226	            lastRefreshedAt = nil
227	        }
228	
229	        return CodexCredentials(
230	            accessToken: accessToken,
231	            accountId: AccountId(rawValue: accountId),
232	            accountEmail: extractAccountEmail(from: tokens),
233	            lastRefreshedAt: lastRefreshedAt
234	        )
235	    }
236	
237	    private static func decodeHexUtf8(_ hex: String) -> String? {
238	        let hexBody = String(hex.dropFirst(2)) // drop "0x"
239	        guard hexBody.count % 2 == 0 else { return nil }
240	        var bytes: [UInt8] = []
241	        var index = hexBody.startIndex
242	        while index < hexBody.endIndex {
243	            let nextIndex = hexBody.index(index, offsetBy: 2)
244	            guard let byte = UInt8(hexBody[index..<nextIndex], radix: 16) else { return nil }
245	            bytes.append(byte)
246	            index = nextIndex
247	        }
248	        return String(bytes: bytes, encoding: .utf8)
249	    }
250	
251	    private func extractAccountEmail(from tokens: [String: Any]?) -> AccountEmail? {
252	        guard let tokens else { return nil }
253	        return extractAccountEmail(fromJWT: tokens["id_token"] as? String)
254	            ?? extractAccountEmail(fromJWT: tokens["access_token"] as? String)
255	    }
256	
257	    private func extractAccountEmail(fromJWT token: String?) -> AccountEmail? {
258	        guard let claims = Self.jwtClaims(from: token) else { return nil }
259	        return normalizedEmail(claims["email"] as? String)
260	            ?? normalizedEmail(claims["https://api.openai.com/profile.email"] as? String)
261	    }
262	
263	    private func normalizedEmail(_ rawValue: String?) -> AccountEmail? {
264	        guard let rawValue else { return nil }
265	        let trimmed = rawValue.trimmingCharacters(in: .whitespacesAndNewlines)
266	        guard !trimmed.isEmpty else { return nil }
267	        return AccountEmail(rawValue: trimmed)
268	    }
269	
270	    private static func jwtClaims(from token: String?) -> [String: Any]? {
271	        guard let token else { return nil }
272	        let segments = token.split(separator: ".", omittingEmptySubsequences: false)
273	        guard segments.count >= 2,
274	              let payload = base64URLDecode(String(segments[1])),
275	              let object = try? JSONSerialization.jsonObject(with: payload),
276	              let claims = object as? [String: Any] else {
277	            return nil
278	        }
279	        return claims
280	    }
281	
282	    private static func base64URLDecode(_ input: String) -> Data? {
283	        let base64 = input
284	            .replacingOccurrences(of: "-", with: "+")
285	            .replacingOccurrences(of: "_", with: "/")
286	        let padding = (4 - (base64.count % 4)) % 4
287	        return Data(base64Encoded: base64 + String(repeating: "=", count: padding))
288	    }
289	}

The function prioritizes the email from the API response, then falls back to the JWT credentials, then checks the cache. When `fetchUsages` succeeds, it returns a single entry marked as active. But here's the pattern: on certain HTTP errors like 429, it returns active with stale metrics, while other errors flip it to inactive. If the Codex API is throwing intermittent errors, the account entry would toggle between active and inactive states with each request.

Let me trace back to `resolveActiveAccount` to understand how multiple Codex accounts are being handled and whether that's where the real issue lies.

content.thinking:
The plugin migration itself didn't introduce the regression, and codex never had `updateIsActive` to begin with. But looking closer at `ClaudeActiveAccountMonitor.checkOnce()`, it's calling `fileManager.updateIsActive(...)` every 15 seconds regardless of whether the condition is actually met.

The codex monitor doesn't do this repair step, so if multiple codex accounts exist in the file, their `isActive` states never get cleaned up. The user says it "alternates" though, which means something's toggling it back and forth. My best hypothesis is that `CodexActiveAccountMonitor.checkOnce()` doesn't repair the active state like Claude's does, leaving stale `isActive: true` entries for accounts that aren't currently running. The alternation pattern suggests the connector is intermittently failing with HTTP errors, which would set `isActive: false` on one account, then the merge logic might be restoring it differently than expected.

Looking at Claude's implementation, it has a monitor that actively repairs `isActive` every 15 seconds based on the actual logged-in account, so even if an error temporarily sets it to false, the monitor restores it. Codex is missing this repair mechanism. Let me check whether codex monitor ever had this capability or if it should be calling `updateIsActive` like Claude does.

content.thinking: Let me shift focus to the feedback issue since it's more straightforward, then I'll circle back to summarize what I found on the first issue. Actually, I should check their usages.json file directly to see what's actually happening in their test build.

Looking at the account statuses in the live file, there's only one active Codex account under fcamblor@gmail.com, which matches the current logged-in user, while the other accounts (Claude and Copilot) are also active but represent different services or email variations.

The user mentions the active marker alternates, so I'm checking what could cause that flip. The Codex connector sets isActive=false on various errors like token_error, api_error, or token_expired, but returns true on success or http_429, which means intermittent errors would toggle the status. Looking at the connector logic, if resolvePersistedAccount returns nil, it returns an empty array and leaves the existing entry untouched, so that wouldn't cause toggling either. Let me check the actual logs to see if there are intermittent failures happening.

content.thinking:
I found the issue—there are multiple timeout errors from the Codex API all occurring within that same timeframe, with the request consistently failing with NSURLErrorDomain Code -1001, which indicates the connection is timing out.

The root cause is that when the Codex API times out, the error handler returns an entry with `isActive: false` by default, and this gets merged into the state. On the next successful fetch, it flips back to `isActive: true`. The real problem is that Codex doesn't have the same repair mechanism that other connectors do—the active-account monitor runs every 15 seconds to fix the state, but the Codex monitor only triggers on actual changes, so it never corrects these transient failures.

Looking at the `errorEntries` function, when it's called for API errors, the `isActive` parameter defaults to false and never gets overridden. The fix is to preserve `isActive: true` for transient errors like timeouts and parse failures when the connector is handling the currently logged-in account, similar to how HTTP 429 errors are already handled correctly.

I should either apply that same pattern to the other transient error cases or add the `updateIsActive` repair loop to the Codex monitor like the other connectors have. Let me check the HTTP 429 case to confirm it's setting `isActive: true` so I can match that approach.

Now I need to look at the second issue about the feedback URL.

content.text: J'ai trouvé l'origine du problème #1. Regardons maintenant le #2.

content.thinking:
I'm mapping out the feedback submission flow: the sheet generates a Markdown comment, then on submit it copies to clipboard, opens the issue page with a fragment to jump to the comment box, and reveals the log file. The user is reporting that only the issue page opens without the other actions happening, so something in that chain is breaking.

Looking at the possibilities—the clipboard copy might be failing silently, or the user doesn't realize it's been set. The footer message explains the full workflow but only appears after submit, so they might not see it. The code does call `copy()` before `openIssue()`, so the clipboard should be filled, but I need to figure out why it's not working as expected. Since GitHub doesn't honor `?body=` parameters on existing issues, the clipboard is the only way to pre-fill the comment, which makes me wonder if the user is actually trying to create a new issue instead and expecting a different workflow.

content.thinking:
The user is reporting that the feedback flow only opens the issue page without executing the other steps. Looking at the sequence, after submission the code should copy to clipboard, open the browser to the issue, reveal the log file in Finder, and then display a footer message — but it seems like only the browser step is happening for them.

I'm wondering if the sheet is dismissing automatically or getting covered by the browser window before the user can see the confirmation message, which would explain why they think nothing else occurred. The clipboard copy should be working based on the code structure, but maybe they're not realizing it happened because there's no visible feedback — a toast notification or alert would make it much clearer that the comment was copied successfully.

I think I've found the root cause—the popover is set up to auto-dismiss whenever it loses focus, and opening the browser window steals that focus away from the menu bar app. So when the user submits and the browser launches, the popover immediately dismisses, taking the sheet with it.

The real problem is that the user never sees confirmation that the comment was copied to the clipboard. The clipboard is actually set correctly, but they have no way of knowing it because the UI feedback disappears before they can read it.

I should show confirmation feedback that persists after the popover closes—either through a system notification, an alert dialog, or by restructuring the flow to confirm the action before opening the browser. The cleanest approach would be to display a clear message like "Comment copied to clipboard! Now opening browser..." with a brief delay before launching, so the user has time to register what happened. Or I could add a confirmation step that lets them review the clipboard content before proceeding to open the browser and finder.

Let me pull together the full analysis and present the findings to the user before implementing any fixes.

content.text:
J'ai bien identifié les deux problèmes. Avant de toucher au code, voici mon analyse :

## Problème #1 — Codex `active` qui clignote

**Cause racine identifiée dans tes logs `~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/codex-usages-connector.log`** : l'API Codex (`https://chatgpt.com/backend-api/wham/usage`) **timeoute périodiquement** :

```
[2026-05-07T15:51:03Z] [ERROR] Codex API request failed: ... The request timed out.
[2026-05-07T16:24:51Z] [ERROR] Codex API request failed: ... The request timed out.
[2026-05-07T16:41:28Z] [ERROR] Codex API request failed: ... The request timed out.
```

À chaque timeout :
1. `CodexConnector.fetchUsages()` → `errorEntries(type: "api_error", credentials:)` → renvoie une entry **avec `isActive: false`** (valeur par défaut, `CodexConnector.swift:298`).
2. Le merge de `UsagesFileManager` remplace l'entry → `isActive` bascule à `false`.
3. Au tick suivant qui réussit → `isActive=true` à nouveau. D'où l'alternance.

**Pourquoi Claude ne fait pas pareil** : `ClaudeActiveAccountMonitor.checkOnce()` (`ClaudeActiveAccountMonitor.swift:66`) appelle `fileManager.updateIsActive(vendor: .claude, activeAccount: ...)` **toutes les 15s**, ce qui *répare* l'état actif en lisant `~/.claude.json`. Le `CodexActiveAccountMonitor` ne fait **pas** cet appel — il ne fait que déclencher un callback sur changement de compte.

**Ce n'est donc pas une régression introduite par la généralisation des plugins** (l'historique git le confirme : le monitor Codex n'a *jamais* fait `updateIsActive`). C'est une **incohérence pré-existante** entre les deux monitors, désormais visible parce que l'API Codex est flaky.

**Deux pistes de correction** (au choix) :
- **A** (symétrie avec Claude) : faire en sorte que `CodexActiveAccountMonitor.checkOnce()` appelle `fileManager.updateIsActive(vendor: .codex, activeAccount: <email résolu depuis l'auth.json/JWT>)`. Le challenge : le monitor a un `accountId`, pas un email. Il faudrait soit déléguer la résolution au connector, soit lire le JWT directement comme dans `CodexCredentialLocator.extractAccountEmail`.
- **B** (plus simple/local) : sur les erreurs **transitoires** (`api_error`, `parse_error`, `http_<5xx>`, `token_error` réseau), préserver `isActive: true` dans l'`errorEntry` quand le compte concerné est celui actuellement loggué — comme on le fait déjà pour `http_429`. Ça garde la cohérence : un timeout réseau ne devrait pas désactiver un compte.

Je recommande **B** : c'est la sémantique correcte (un timeout n'est pas un "compte inactif"), c'est local au connector, et ça aligne avec le comportement existant du 429.

## Problème #2 — Feedback : seul le browser s'ouvre

**Le clipboard EST déjà copié** dans le code actuel (`TesterFeedbackSheet.submit()` à la ligne 263) :

```swift
private func submit() {
    let body = renderedComment
    submitter.copy(body)           // ← clipboard filled
    submitter.openIssue(...)        // ← browser opens
    submitter.reveal(...)           // ← Finder reveals log
    didSubmit = true                // ← footer message shown
}
```

**Mais** : l'app vit dans un `NSPopover` (menu bar). Quand `NSWorkspace.shared.open(url)` lance le navigateur, le popover **perd le focus et se referme automatiquement** (comportement `.transient` par défaut). L'utilisateur ne voit donc jamais le message de confirmation `"Comment copied. Browser opened on the issue. Drag the highlighted log file..."`. Le clipboard est rempli, mais l'utilisateur n'a aucun feedback visuel et croit que rien ne s'est passé.

GitHub n'honore pas `?body=` sur les URLs d'issue existante (commentaire déjà documenté dans le code), donc le pré-remplissage URL est impossible — c'est une vraie limite GitHub.

**Pistes de correction** :
- **A** (la moins invasive) : afficher un `NSAlert` modal **avant** d'ouvrir le navigateur, qui dit explicitement "Comment copied to clipboard. Click Continue to open the issue and reveal the log file." → l'utilisateur voit clairement ce qui se passe, et ne perd pas l'info quand le popover se ferme.
- **B** : afficher une notification système (`UNUserNotificationCenter`) en plus, pour confirmation persistante.
- **C** : ajouter un état "copied, ready to open" intermédiaire dans la sheet : premier clic = copie + reveal, deuxième clic = ouvre le navigateur. Ça donne le temps à l'utilisateur de lire avant la perte de focus.

Je recommande **A + C** combinés : l'`NSAlert` survit à la fermeture du popover (présenté sur l'app, pas dans la popover), et ça reste très simple.

**Veux-tu que je parte sur la piste B pour le #1 et A pour le #2 ?** Ou tu préfères d'autres options ? Une fois validé, j'écris le code + tests.
Notification reçue, sans impact sur l'analyse. J'attends ta validation des pistes proposées (B pour #1, A+C pour #2) avant d'implémenter.

content:
fais B pour le probleme #1
fais A+C pour le problème #2 (et copie le contenu du fichier de log sous forme d'un log avec triple backquotes dans le clipboard)

Let me start with the simpler fix first.

content.thinking:
I'm refining the error handling logic to distinguish between transient errors that should preserve stale metrics with `isActive: true` versus permanent failures that warrant `isActive: false`. The `token_error` case is tricky since it could mean either the user logged out or the auth file is being rewritten during login, so without more context I'll keep it as `isActive: false` to be safe.

For errors that occur after we've already loaded credentials, we know the current account is authenticated, so I should preserve `isActive: true` across `api_error`, `parse_error`, and 5xx responses. The key insight is that any error happening post-credentials means we've confirmed the account is logged in, so I'll update the code to reflect that.

1	import Foundation
2	
3	/// Activation context for the in-app tester feedback affordance described
4	/// in `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` §6.4.
5	///
6	/// The feedback UI is visible iff **all three** Info.plist keys are
7	/// present and parseable in the running bundle:
8	///
9	/// - `AITrackerVendorDebug` — vendor slug under test (also drives
10	///   `VerboseVendorMode`).
11	/// - `AITrackerBuildCommit` — full 40-char commit SHA the DMG was built
12	///   from.
13	/// - `AITrackerOnboardingIssueURL` — fully-qualified GitHub issue URL
14	///   the tester should comment on.
15	///
16	/// All-or-nothing semantics are deliberate: a stable release MUST NOT
17	/// expose this affordance, and the cleanest way to enforce that at
18	/// runtime is to gate the entire UI on the presence of every key. The
19	/// release workflow asserts the trio is absent from any tagged build;
20	/// this resolver makes a partial set fail closed.
21	public struct TesterFeedbackContext: Sendable, Equatable {
22	    public let vendor: Vendor
23	    public let buildCommit: String
24	    public let issueURL: URL
25	
26	    public static let vendorPlistKey = "AITrackerVendorDebug"
27	    public static let buildCommitPlistKey = "AITrackerBuildCommit"
28	    public static let issueURLPlistKey = "AITrackerOnboardingIssueURL"
29	
30	    /// Cached resolution against the running app's main bundle. `nil` in
31	    /// tests (the test bundle's Info.plist does not carry the keys) and
32	    /// in stable releases by construction.
33	    public static let resolved: TesterFeedbackContext? = resolve(bundle: .main)
34	
35	    public init(vendor: Vendor, buildCommit: String, issueURL: URL) {
36	        self.vendor = vendor
37	        self.buildCommit = buildCommit
38	        self.issueURL = issueURL
39	    }
40	
41	    /// Test-friendly resolver. Returns `nil` if any key is missing,
42	    /// empty after trimming, or shaped unexpectedly (build commit must
43	    /// be 40 hex chars; issue URL must be the canonical
44	    /// `https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/issues/<n>` form).
45	    public static func resolve(bundle: Bundle?) -> TesterFeedbackContext? {
46	        guard let bundle else { return nil }
47	        guard let vendorRaw = string(bundle, vendorPlistKey),
48	              let commitRaw = string(bundle, buildCommitPlistKey),
49	              let urlRaw = string(bundle, issueURLPlistKey) else {
50	            return nil
51	        }
52	        guard isFullCommitSha(commitRaw),
53	              let url = canonicalIssueURL(urlRaw) else {
54	            return nil
55	        }
56	        return TesterFeedbackContext(
57	            vendor: Vendor(rawValue: vendorRaw),
58	            buildCommit: commitRaw,
59	            issueURL: url
60	        )
61	    }
62	
63	    public var shortCommit: String {
64	        String(buildCommit.prefix(8))
65	    }
66	
67	    /// Resolved per-vendor connector log path the tester is asked to
68	    /// attach to the issue comment. Mirrors the path used by
69	    /// `Logger.<vendor>` in production.
70	    public func connectorLogURL(home: URL = FileManager.default.homeDirectoryForCurrentUser) -> URL {
71	        home
72	            .appendingPathComponent(".cache", isDirectory: true)
73	            .appendingPathComponent("ai-usages-tracker", isDirectory: true)
74	            .appendingPathComponent("\(vendor.rawValue)-usages-connector.log", isDirectory: false)
75	    }
76	
77	    private static func string(_ bundle: Bundle, _ key: String) -> String? {
78	        guard let raw = bundle.object(forInfoDictionaryKey: key) as? String else {
79	            return nil
80	        }
81	        let trimmed = raw.trimmingCharacters(in: .whitespacesAndNewlines)
82	        return trimmed.isEmpty ? nil : trimmed
83	    }
84	
85	    private static func isFullCommitSha(_ value: String) -> Bool {
86	        guard value.count == fullCommitShaLength else { return false }
87	        return value.allSatisfy { $0.isHexDigit }
88	    }
89	
90	    private static let fullCommitShaLength = 40
91	
92	    private static func canonicalIssueURL(_ value: String) -> URL? {
93	        // Pattern: https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/issues/<n>
94	        guard let url = URL(string: value),
95	              url.scheme == "https",
96	              url.host == "github.com" else {
97	            return nil
98	        }
99	        let parts = url.path.split(separator: "/", omittingEmptySubsequences: true)
100	        guard parts.count == 4,
101	              parts[2] == "issues",
102	              Int(parts[3]) != nil else {
103	            return nil
104	        }
105	        return url
106	    }
107	}
108

Let me think through how the Submit button should handle this flow. I'm working through the flow here. The user wants both the alert (A) and the intermediate sheet state (C) combined. So the cleanest approach is: first click copies the comment with log to clipboard and reveals the log file, then the sheet updates to show a confirmation message with a button that says "Open issue". When the user clicks that button, an NSAlert appears asking if they're ready to open GitHub, reminding them the comment is already copied. Once they confirm, the browser opens.

I'm sketching out the implementation with a phase state that tracks whether we're still composing, have copied, or are done. The submit action will handle the copy and reveal, update the phase, and then show the alert that survives the popover closing.

Building the state machine... I'm splitting the submit flow into two phases: first copy and reveal the log, then open the issue with a confirmation dialog. 1	import Foundation
2	
3	/// Pure builder for the tester sign-off comment Markdown described in
4	/// `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` §3.1.5. Decoupled from any UI / clipboard
5	/// concern so the formatting can be unit-tested without AppKit.
6	public struct TesterFeedbackComment: Sendable {
7	    public struct Input: Sendable {
8	        /// Self-declared subscription tier on the account being tested.
9	        public enum Plan: String, Sendable, CaseIterable {
10	            case free, pro, team, enterprise, other
11	        }
12	
13	        /// One Verified-checklist item from the sign-off template.
14	        public struct ChecklistItem: Sendable {
15	            public let label: String
16	            public let confirmed: Bool
17	
18	            public init(label: String, confirmed: Bool) {
19	                self.label = label
20	                self.confirmed = confirmed
21	            }
22	        }
23	
24	        public let plan: Plan
25	        public let planOtherDescription: String?
26	        public let macOSVersion: String
27	        public let context: TesterFeedbackContext
28	        public let submissionPath: SubmissionPath
29	        public let checklist: [ChecklistItem]
30	        public let connectorLogAttached: Bool
31	        public let notes: String
32	
33	        public init(
34	            plan: Plan,
35	            planOtherDescription: String? = nil,
36	            macOSVersion: String,
37	            context: TesterFeedbackContext,
38	            submissionPath: SubmissionPath,
39	            checklist: [ChecklistItem],
40	            connectorLogAttached: Bool,
41	            notes: String = ""
42	        ) {
43	            self.plan = plan
44	            self.planOtherDescription = planOtherDescription
45	            self.macOSVersion = macOSVersion
46	            self.context = context
47	            self.submissionPath = submissionPath
48	            self.checklist = checklist
49	            self.connectorLogAttached = connectorLogAttached
50	            self.notes = notes
51	        }
52	    }
53	
54	    /// Path the tester used to produce the comment. Recorded in the
55	    /// rendered body so the maintainer can correlate UX bugs with the
56	    /// in-app flow.
57	    public enum SubmissionPath: String, Sendable {
58	        case inApp = "in-app"
59	        case manual
60	    }
61	
62	    public static let sentinel = "✅ tester-confirm"
63	    public static let notesCharacterCap = 2_048
64	
65	    public static func render(_ input: Input) -> String {
66	        var lines: [String] = [sentinel, ""]
67	        lines.append("Plan: \(planLine(input))")
68	        lines.append("macOS: \(input.macOSVersion)")
69	        lines.append("Build SHA: \(input.context.shortCommit) (full: \(input.context.buildCommit))")
70	        lines.append("Submission path: \(input.submissionPath.rawValue)")
71	        lines.append("Verified:")
72	        for item in input.checklist {
73	            let marker = item.confirmed ? "x" : " "
74	            lines.append("- [\(marker)] \(item.label)")
75	        }
76	        lines.append("Connector log attached: \(input.connectorLogAttached ? "yes" : "no")")
77	        let trimmedNotes = String(input.notes.prefix(notesCharacterCap))
78	            .trimmingCharacters(in: .whitespacesAndNewlines)
79	        if !trimmedNotes.isEmpty {
80	            lines.append("Notes: \(trimmedNotes)")
81	        }
82	        return lines.joined(separator: "\n")
83	    }
84	
85	    private static func planLine(_ input: Input) -> String {
86	        switch input.plan {
87	        case .free:       return "Free"
88	        case .pro:        return "Pro"
89	        case .team:       return "Team"
90	        case .enterprise: return "Enterprise"
91	        case .other:
92	            let description = (input.planOtherDescription ?? "")
93	                .trimmingCharacters(in: .whitespacesAndNewlines)
94	            return description.isEmpty ? "Other" : "Other: \(description)"
95	        }
96	    }
97	}

content.text:
## Résumé des corrections

### Problème #1 — Codex `active` qui clignote (piste B)
`CodexConnector.swift` : sur les erreurs **transitoires** (timeout réseau `api_error`, parse `parse_error`, erreurs HTTP autres que 401/429), je préserve maintenant `isActive: true` + les `lastKnownMetrics` (même pattern que le HTTP 429 existant). Un timeout de l'API ChatGPT ne désactive plus le compte. Le `token_expired` (HTTP 401) reste à `isActive: false` — c'est volontaire, l'utilisateur doit se relogguer.

3 nouveaux tests dans `CodexConnectorTests.swift` (api_error / parse_error / http_503) qui chacun seedent des metrics, déclenchent l'erreur et vérifient `isActive == true` + metrics conservées.

### Problème #2 — Feedback feedback (piste A + C, plus log dans le clipboard)
- **Étape C (intermediate state)** : le bouton "Submit" est devenu une machine à 2 états :
  - **`composing`** → libellé "Copy & reveal log" — copie le commentaire **+ tail du log dans un block ` ```log ` ** dans le clipboard et révèle le fichier dans Finder. La sheet reste ouverte avec un message clair.
  - **`copied`** → libellé "Open GitHub issue" — déclenche l'étape A.
- **Étape A (NSAlert)** : nouveau `confirmOpenIssue(title:message:)` ajouté au protocole `TesterFeedbackSubmitting`. L'implémentation système ouvre un `NSAlert` modal qui **survit à la fermeture du popover** (le NSAlert s'attache à l'app, pas à la popover). Sur "Open issue" → `NSWorkspace.open(...)`.
- **Log dans le clipboard** : nouvelle méthode `TesterFeedbackComment.renderWithLogTail(_:logFileContents:tailByteCap:)` qui ajoute en fin de commentaire un block ` ```log\n…\n``` `, capé à 30 KB (limite GitHub 65 KB) et snap sur frontière de ligne pour ne jamais couper en milieu de ligne. La sheet lit le fichier au moment du Submit ; si la lecture échoue, le commentaire est envoyé sans log.

5 nouveaux tests dans `TesterFeedbackCommentTests.swift` couvrent : log présent / log vide / cap respecté / petite taille / snap ligne.

**Build OK + 505 tests verts**. Les diagnostics SourceKit "no member 'renderWithLogTail'" sont des faux-positifs d'index (le compilateur lui-même valide tout).

content.text:
# Smart commit

Tu dois committer les changements git en cours dans le repo courant en suivant les guidelines ci-dessous.

## Contexte utilisateur

Intent fourni par l'utilisateur (peut être vide) : ``

## Étapes

### 1. Analyser l'état du repo

Lance en parallèle :
- `git status` (sans `-uall`)
- `git diff --staged` et `git diff` pour voir les changements
- `git log -n 5 --pretty=format:'%h %s%n%b%n---'` pour voir les 4-5 derniers commits

### 2. Détecter les conventions du projet

À partir des 4-5 derniers commits :
- **Conventional commits ?** Si la majorité suit le format `type(scope): message` (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, build, ci...) → utilise ce format. Sinon → format libre.
- **Langue ?** Détecte la langue dominante des messages (français, anglais, autre) → utilise la même langue. En cas de doute, français.
- **Style ?** Impératif vs descriptif, majuscule initiale ou non, présence de scope, etc. → imite.

### 3. Détecter un éventuel fixup

Si l'intent utilisateur mentionne un correctif (mots-clés : "fix", "corrige", "répare", "bug", "issue", "problème", "régression", "broken"...) :

1. Identifie les fichiers modifiés (`git diff --name-only HEAD` + fichiers staged).
2. Pour chaque fichier, regarde `git log -n 10 --oneline -- <fichier>` pour voir quels commits récents l'ont touché.
3. Si **un seul commit récent** (dans les ~5 derniers, non encore pushé sur main/master partagé — vérifie avec `git log @{u}..HEAD` si upstream) est clairement à l'origine du code cassé, propose un **`git commit --fixup=<sha>`** au lieu d'un commit classique.
4. Si ambigu ou si le commit date d'avant `@{u}` (déjà pushé en amont partagé), reste sur un commit classique.

Ne fais **pas** de rebase autosquash automatiquement — laisse ce soin à l'utilisateur.

### 4. Rédiger le message

Règles impératives :
- **Le message explique le POURQUOI**, pas le QUOI. Le diff dit déjà ce qui change. Le message dit pourquoi ce changement existe (motivation, contrainte, problème résolu, contexte métier).
- Sujet court (≤72 caractères), en suivant la convention détectée.
- Si le pourquoi ne tient pas dans le sujet, ajoute un corps (ligne vide + explication). Sinon, pas de corps inutile.
- Pas de name-dropping de fichiers/fonctions dans le sujet — le diff suffit.
- Pas de phrases vides genre « Mise à jour du code » ou « Divers fixes ».

Si l'intent utilisateur est vide ou trop vague pour déduire le pourquoi, et que le diff ne le rend pas évident, **demande à l'utilisateur** avant de committer plutôt que d'inventer.

### 5. Staging et commit

- Si rien n'est staged, stage les fichiers pertinents **par nom** (jamais `git add -A` ni `git add .` pour éviter d'embarquer secrets/binaires).
- Ignore les fichiers suspects (`.env`, `credentials*`, clés privées, dumps volumineux). Préviens l'utilisateur si tu en vois.
- Crée le commit via HEREDOC pour préserver le formatage multiligne.
- **Ne jamais** utiliser `--no-verify`, `--amend` (sauf demande explicite), ni `--no-gpg-sign`.
- Si un hook pre-commit échoue : diagnostique la cause, corrige, re-stage, et crée un **nouveau** commit (pas d'amend).

### 6. Vérification finale

Après commit, lance `git status` et `git log -1` pour confirmer. Reporte brièvement : sha court + sujet + (fixup le cas échéant).

## Garde-fous

- Ne push jamais.
- Ne crée pas de commit vide.
- Ne touche pas à `git config`.
- Si plusieurs "unités logiques" distinctes sont mélangées dans le diff, préviens l'utilisateur et propose de splitter plutôt que de tout entasser. Tu peux utiliser `git stash` (avec `-u`/`--keep-index` si pertinent) pour isoler des sous-ensembles — mais cette commande n'est **pas** auto-approuvée, l'utilisateur devra confirmer à chaque usage : ne l'utilise que si c'est vraiment nécessaire.

1. D'abord, voir l'état du repo avec git status, diff, et log récents.
2. Détecter les conventions du projet (conventional commits, langue, style).
3. Vérifier s'il y a un fixup à faire (unlikely here, c'est une nouvelle feature/fix).
4. Rédiger un message approprié.
5. Stage les fichiers pertinents et créer le commit.
6. Vérifier avec git status et git log.

Je dois déterminer:
- Conventional commits? Je dois regarder les 4-5 derniers commits.
- Langue? Probablement français ou anglais selon le pattern détecté.
- POURQUOI ce changement. L'utilisateur a dit: "fais B pour le probleme #1" et "fais A+C pour le problème #2". C'est deux corrections à deux régressions identifiées.

Laisse-moi commencer par les vérifications.

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user reported two bugs in an AI usage tracker macOS app during testing:
   - Bug #1: Codex account's "active" status alternates/flickers between active and inactive
   - Bug #2: Submitting tester feedback simply opens the GitHub issue page without providing confirmation that the comment was copied to clipboard
   
   User requested specific fixes:
   - For #1: Use approach "B" = preserve `isActive: true` on transient network/API errors (like existing http_429 handling), not just on success
   - For #2: Use approach "A+C" = show NSAlert modal confirmation before opening browser + implement 2-phase submit UI + include connector log file content in clipboard with triple backticks

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Transient vs permanent errors in API connectors (transient shouldn't change active account state)
   - Actor isolation (@MainActor) for UI operations that must survive popover dismissal
   - File I/O with byte capping and line boundary snapping
   - Two-phase UI state machine pattern
   - Fenced code blocks for embedding log content
   - Metrics preservation across error states

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - **AIUsagesTrackers/Sources/AIUsagesTrackers/Connectors/CodexConnector.swift**
     - Modified `api_error` path (line 84-92): Now calls `errorEntries(type: "api_error", credentials: credentials, isActive: true, preservedMetrics: lastKnownMetrics)` instead of default `isActive: false`
     - Modified `http_<other>` path (line 118-126): Now calls `errorEntries(type: "http_\(httpCode)", credentials: credentials, isActive: true, preservedMetrics: lastKnownMetrics)`
     - Modified `parse_error` path (line 156-165): Now calls `errorEntries(type: "parse_error", credentials: credentials, isActive: true, preservedMetrics: lastKnownMetrics)`
     - Added comments explaining transient error semantics

   - **AIUsagesTrackers/Sources/AIUsagesTrackers/Plugins/TesterFeedbackComment.swift**
     - Added `logTailByteCap` constant = 30,000 bytes
     - Added `renderWithLogTail(_:logFileContents:tailByteCap:)` method that appends log as `Connector log tail:\n```log\n...\n```"
     - Added `trimmedLogTail(_:byteCap:)` static method that caps UTF-8 byte count and snaps to line boundaries

   - **AIUsagesTrackers/Sources/App/Views/TesterFeedbackSheet.swift**
     - Removed `@State private var didSubmit: Bool = false`
     - Added `@State private var phase: SubmitPhase = .composing` with enum: `case composing, case copied`
     - Added `renderedCommentWithLog()` method that reads connector log file and calls `renderWithLogTail()`
     - Renamed `submit()` to `primaryAction()` with 2-phase logic: composing phase copies+reveals then sets state to copied; copied phase shows NSAlert then opens browser
     - Updated footer to show different messages: composing shows button "Copy & reveal log"; copied shows "Comment + connector log tail copied..." with button "Open GitHub issue"
     - Updated protocol `TesterFeedbackSubmitting` to add `@MainActor func confirmOpenIssue(title: String, message: String) -> Bool`
     - Implemented in `SystemTesterFeedbackSubmitter`: creates NSAlert with title, message, "Open issue" and "Cancel" buttons, returns whether user clicked Open

   - **AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/CodexConnectorTests.swift**
     - Updated test "malformed JSON payload returns parse_error entry" to "malformed JSON payload returns parse_error entry, preserves isActive and metrics"
       - Now verifies `entries[0].isActive == true` and `entries[0].metrics == firstEntries[0].metrics`
     - Updated test "URLError returns api_error entry" to "URLError returns api_error entry, preserves isActive and metrics"
       - Now verifies `entries[0].isActive == true` and `entries[0].metrics == firstEntries[0].metrics`
     - Added new test "HTTP 5xx preserves isActive and metrics across server hiccups"
       - Seeds metrics with successful fetch, triggers HTTP 503, verifies `isActive == true` and metrics preserved

   - **AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/TesterFeedbackCommentTests.swift**
     - Added test "renderWithLogTail appends a fenced ```log block when the log is non-empty"
     - Added test "renderWithLogTail omits the log block when contents are blank"
     - Added test "trimmedLogTail caps to byteCap and snaps to a line boundary"
       - Verifies 100 lines of "0123456789" are truncated but remain complete lines
     - Added test "trimmedLogTail returns the full content when smaller than byteCap"

4. Errors and fixes:
   - Actor isolation warnings in confirmOpenIssue: Fixed by adding `@MainActor` annotation to both protocol method and implementation
   - SourceKit diagnostics showing "no member 'renderWithLogTail'" after initial edits: Resolved automatically on rebuild; stale index issue

5. Problem Solving:
   - Problem #1 (Codex flicker): Root cause was inconsistency between Claude and Codex monitors. Claude repairs `isActive` state via `updateIsActive()` every 15s regardless of errors. Codex never called this. Solution applied error handling pattern already established in http_429 case to all transient errors.
   - Problem #2 (Feedback UX): Root cause was popover auto-dismissing when browser opens, losing confirmation message. Solution used two-phase UI so first phase completes in-sheet, then modal alert (MainActor, not popover-bound) shows before browser opens.
